artifacts.content:
Hi Claire,

I’m hiding in the car outside Mom and Dad’s for a minute before I go in and start the nightly meds-and-dinner routine. Three to four evenings a week in Camp Hill is catching up with me, especially on days when I’ve already bounced between Bellaire, Mooreland, and the IU. Since her early-stage Alzheimer’s diagnosis at Holy Spirit back in October, I feel like every pill organizer I fill is this little reminder that the clock is moving faster than we want.

Tonight I brought over another stack of those picture labels for the bathroom and kitchen cabinets. The neurologist at Holy Spirit keeps saying structure will help, which makes sense—it’s basically what we preach for our 4-year-olds, just in reverse. I keep thinking about our kinship caregivers when I’m here; Dad is essentially doing a grandparent caregiver role without the little kids.

On top of that, I had to sign the $12,800 repayment plan with the district business office this week for the time-and-effort audit mess from 2022–23. Twenty-four months of a smaller paycheck because I mis-coded about five hours a week between Title I and Pre-K Counts. It’s mortifying, and of course I keep replaying every spreadsheet I’ve ever touched.

Meanwhile, James had another rough morning—his counselor at Bellaire thinks the ADHD meds might need a tweak. Between his weekly school-based sessions (ever since that March 2022 UPMC Children’s diagnosis) and Mom’s neurology appointments, my calendar looks like a medical bingo card.

Anyway, I’m rambling. How did Emily do at Head Start today? Text me if you need me to pick her up later this week; I’m already halfway to Harrisburg most nights.

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: Quick brain dump before I go in

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – late night kitchen table, 3/12/2024

I keep circling back to how many different systems we’re touching as a family right now and how much that shapes the way I show up at work.

James: ADHD diagnosis from UPMC Children’s in Pittsburgh last March after that six-month stretch of impulsivity and aggressive outbursts in Bellaire’s Pre-K Counts room. Now weekly school-based counseling since Fall 2022 and an IEP that I can practically recite. I spend my days coaching other parents through this and then come home and color-code his behavior charts on our own fridge.

Mom: early-stage Alzheimer’s, Holy Spirit, October 2023. Three–four evenings a week driving to Oakwood Lane, setting up meds, walking her through the same bedtime routine. The six-week adult day program trial over the winter helped a little, but Dad is still exhausted.

Me: two rounds of therapy in the last few years—first with Dr. McKay at Carlisle Counseling Center from August 2020 through June 2021 (every other Thursday, 4–4:50 p.m.) when I was drowning in remote Pre-K coordination and the boys’ virtual schooling, and then last year’s weekly telehealth with Karen Patel at Penn State Health in Camp Hill (May–October 2022) when the burnout slid into actual depression. The word still feels heavy, but accurate.

Some days I wonder how many of our teachers are quietly juggling similar lists and just don’t have the language or access to supports. If we’re going to talk about “quality” in Pre-K Counts and HSSAP, staff mental health has to be in the definition, not a footnote.

Hi Jen,

I owe you a fun update about book club, but tonight you’re getting the messy version of my brain.

Work has been…a lot. I keep flashing back to October 2021, sitting in Mark Ellison’s office at Bellaire while he “suggested” I bump a homeless 4-year-old off our Pre-K Counts roster to make room for a “more reliable” tuition-paying family. Filing that grievance with HR and looping in the union rep was exhausting, but honestly it was the only thing that kept me from quitting on the spot. That kid is exactly who PKC exists for.

Then there was the 2020–21 year, when the district business office replied to my November 2020 email asking for emergency funds with a flat “no,” so I ended up putting about $1,200 of Giant and Sheetz gift cards on our personal Visa for three Pre-K families who kept showing up hungry or out of gas. This February’s time-and-effort audit and the $12,800 repayment plan over 24 months feels like salt in the wound. I make one coding mistake—about five hours a week mis-assigned between Title I and PKC—and I’m on a payment plan, but there’s no mechanism to reimburse the grocery cards.

Layer all of that on top of the Noble Boulevard domestic incident case I shared on the South Central Early Learning Council call back in Fall 2020—the Head Start dad’s arrest in September, CYF and Carlisle PD in the mix, the child suddenly with a kinship caregiver—and it just feels like the system is permanently balanced on the backs of the most fragile people.

Anyway, that’s my cheery Tuesday night. How’s your corner of the world?

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: Why I’m so tired of “the system” this week

artifacts.content:
Event: Therapy – telehealth with Karen P.
When: Wednesdays, 7:00–7:50 p.m., recurring May 11, 2022 – October 26, 2022
Location: Penn State Health behavioral health – Camp Hill (telehealth from home office)
Attendees: Danielle + Karen Patel, LCSW

Notes:

• Focus: burnout/depression related to juggling Early Childhood Program Coordinator role, Ethan’s remote days at Lamberton Middle during COVID disruptions, James’s transition out of Pre-K Counts at Bellaire, and constant fear of PKC program cuts.
• Remember to talk about the lingering guilt from using our personal credit card for ~$1,200 in grocery/gas cards for three PKC families in 2020–21 after the business office denied emergency funds in that November 2020 email.
• Also want to unpack February 2023, when I took three weeks of FMLA to be Emily’s primary caregiver at 214 Willow Run after Ms. Rodriguez called from her Dauphin County Head Start classroom about the Children & Youth neglect investigation into Claire’s then-partner. Head Start’s rigid attendance rules during that active child welfare case still make my stomach knot.
• Check in on how I’m carrying stories like the Noble Boulevard domestic incident case (dad’s arrest Sept 2020, kid moved to kinship caregiver) that I shared on the PA Early Learning Council South Central workgroup call in Fall 2020—what’s healthy advocacy vs. vicarious trauma?
• Do NOT schedule evening United Way or Equity Committee meetings over this.

Mom,

I wanted to write this down for you since it’s hard to cover everything in quick phone calls.

Emily is doing a little better this week. Claire said her Head Start teacher, Ms. Rodriguez, hasn’t had to call about any new incidents, which is a relief after last year. I still think about February 2023, when Ms. R. first called to say Children & Youth had opened that neglect investigation into Claire’s ex. Those three weeks when Emily stayed with us on Willow Run while Claire was back and forth to the Dauphin County Human Services Building were some of the hardest I’ve had as an aunt—and as a working mom. I’m glad I could take FMLA, but I wish the Head Start attendance system had been less rigid while everything with the caseworker was unfolding.

On our side of the family circus, James’s ADHD supports at Bellaire seem to be helping. Since the March 2022 evaluation at UPMC Children’s in Pittsburgh, his school-based counseling has made mornings a bit less explosive. Ethan’s just stressed about Lamberton band and too much homework.

I’m still driving to Camp Hill three–four evenings a week to help you and Dad, and I’m glad the neurologist at Holy Spirit caught the Alzheimer’s early. The six-week adult day program trial over the winter seemed to perk you up a bit, even if you don’t remember all the activities.

Please don’t worry about me “doing too much.” I am tired, yes, but I also have more tools now from therapy (remember the counselor in Carlisle during COVID, and then Karen in Camp Hill last year). I’d rather be in the middle of all this with you than watching from a distance.

Love you,
Danielle

metadata.subject: Quick update on the kids and Emily

artifacts.content:
Running list – situations shaping how I think about “family-friendly” policy (for my own clarity)

1. James at Bellaire’s PA Pre-K Counts classroom (Sept 2019–June 2021): anxiety and selective mutism spiraled after Michael’s layoff from the Mechanicsburg plant in March 2020. Weekly drives to UPMC Children’s outpatient clinic in Harrisburg to see Dr. Melissa Grant, coordinating with his Pre-K teacher and the mental health consultant to build that classroom plan. Watching them adapt circle time so he could participate without being forced to speak still makes me teary.

2. Emily’s world: porch preschool in Summer 2020 at 19 Oakwood Lane when her Dauphin County Head Start center closed for COVID, weaving in the social stories and visual schedules her developmental pediatrician at Hershey recommended after her May 2020 autism diagnosis. Fast forward to the May 2021 custody hearing in Dauphin County—I wrote that support letter for Claire, documenting how Emily’s behavior tanked after missed visits with her dad and how her Harrisburg Head Start teachers reported sky-high separation anxiety.

3. Claire’s crisis Feb 2023: Ms. Rodriguez’s call from the Dauphin County Head Start room about the neglect investigation, Emily moving into our townhouse for three weeks, and Claire shuttling between work and the Human Services Building.

All of this sits in the back of my mind when I’m in meetings about PKC/HSSAP “compliance.” These aren’t hypotheticals; they’re our kids.

metadata.title: Why I care so much about policy – family cases

artifacts.content:
Hey Pastor Mark,

I’ve been meaning to send a proper debrief now that the spring parenting series at St. John’s is a few years in the rearview, especially as we talk about possibly reviving something similar.

I still think about those eight consecutive Tuesday evenings in Spring 2019—6:30–8:30 p.m., kids’ group in that little classroom off the fellowship hall while the Cumberland County Children & Youth–referred parents met down the hall. We usually had 4–6 children, but the one who has stayed with me is the 5-year-old whose dad was incarcerated at SCI Camp Hill and whose mom was working through a reunification plan.

Coordinating with his CYS caseworker and Dr. Melissa Grant, the therapist contracted for the series, to build attachment-focused activities was some of the most meaningful work I’ve done outside my district role. I remember getting informal updates about court dates and supervised visits so I could anticipate his behavior the week after a hearing. It blurred the line between “volunteer” and “team member,” but it also made the difference between a chaotic evening and one where he could actually practice naming feelings instead of acting them out.

That experience sits alongside others in my head: the Drug Treatment Court playgroup at Carlisle UMC that Claire roped me into in 2022–23; the little unlicensed co-op we ran on Willow Run during hybrid schooling in early 2021; even the Carlisle Family Stability Collaborative board meetings at United Way where we weighed PPL shutoff notices against limited funds.

If we do another series, I’d love us to think intentionally about boundaries and supports for volunteers, given how heavy some of the stories are.

Peace,
Danielle
